questions.question: Bal Gangadhar Tilak, a prominent extremist leader, wrote the newspaper 'Mahratta' in which language?
questions.solution:
Newspapers of Bal Gangadhar Tilak

Bal Gangadhar Tilak, a central figure in the 'Extremist' faction of the Indian National Congress, published two prominent newspapers to spread nationalist ideas:

  • Mahratta: Published in English.
  • Kesari: Published in Marathi.

Through these journals, Tilak criticized British policies and advocated for Swaraj (Self-rule).

questions.question: At which session of the Indian National Congress was the resolution for Poorna Swaraj (complete independence) passed?
questions.solution:
Poorna Swaraj Resolution (1929)

The Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress in 1929 was a historic turning point. Under the presidency of Jawaharlal Nehru, the Congress passed the Poorna Swaraj (Complete Independence) resolution, shifting the goal from Dominion Status to total independence from British rule.

  • The Congress decided to observe January 26, 1930, as 'Independence Day'.
  • The Tri-colour flag was hoisted on the banks of the river Ravi.

questions.question: Hunger strike as a weapon was used for the first time by Mahatma Gandhi during which struggle?
questions.solution:
Ahmedabad Mill Workers' Struggle (1918)

Mahatma Gandhi used hunger strike as a political weapon for the first time during the Ahmedabad Mill Workers' Struggle in 1918. He intervened in a dispute between the mill owners and workers regarding the 'Plague Bonus'. The strike ended successfully with the workers receiving a 35% wage increase.

questions.question: Which Act empowered Her Majesty to issue Letters Patent for setting up High Courts?
questions.solution:
Indian High Courts Act, 1861

The Indian High Courts Act of 1861 empowered the British Crown to issue Letters Patent to establish High Courts in the Presidency towns. This led to the creation of the High Courts of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay in 1862, replacing the old Supreme Courts and Sadar Adalats.

questions.question: Who among the following is NOT considered a leader of the Moderates in the Indian National Congress?
questions.solution:
Moderates vs. Extremists

The early phase of the INC was dominated by the 'Moderates'. Aurobindo Ghosh, however, was a prominent leader of the Extremist faction, along with the 'Lal-Bal-Pal' trio. He was a vocal critic of the Moderate 'policy of petitions' and later became a spiritual philosopher.

FactionKey Leaders
ModeratesDadabhai Naoroji, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Ferozshah Mehta, W.C. Bonnerjee
ExtremistsBal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, Bipin Chandra Pal, Aurobindo Ghosh

questions.question: In 1892, the Indian National Congress passed a significant resolution at its Allahabad Session, which was a demand for the inclusion of Indians in which of the following?
questions.solution:
Allahabad Session (1892)

During the Allahabad Session of 1892, the INC reiterated its demand for the expansion and reform of the Legislative Councils. They sought more Indian representation and the right to discuss the budget, which eventually influenced the provisions of the Indian Councils Act of 1892.

questions.question: What was the main issue debated during the 1905 Banaras Session of the INC, right after the announcement of Bengal’s partition?
questions.option_1: Immediate resignation of British administrators in Bengal
questions.option_3: Formation of All India Muslim League
questions.solution:
Banaras Session (1905)

The 1905 Banaras Session, presided over by Gopal Krishna Gokhale, occurred immediately after the Partition of Bengal. The session was marked by intense debate over the formal adoption of Swadeshi (use of Indian goods) and the Boycott of British goods as a protest against the partition.

questions.question: During the 1886 session of the Indian National Congress in Calcutta, a major demand was raised for simultaneous Indian Civil Service examinations in England and India. Who presided over this session?
questions.solution:
Calcutta Session (1886)

The second session of the INC was held in Calcutta in 1886 and was presided over by Dadabhai Naoroji. A key resolution was the demand for holding simultaneous Civil Service examinations in both India and England to give Indians a fair chance to enter the administration.

questions.question: Which leader presided over the 1929 Lahore Session of the Indian National Congress, where the resolution for 'Purna Swaraj' was passed?
questions.solution:
Presidency of the Lahore Session

Jawaharlal Nehru presided over the landmark 1929 Lahore Session. His election symbolized the rise of the younger, more radical generation within the Congress that was no longer satisfied with slow reforms and demanded immediate, complete independence.

questions.question: What role did the Indian People’s Theatre Association (IPTA) play during the British rule?
questions.solution:
Indian People’s Theatre Association (IPTA)

The IPTA was a cultural wing of the anti-colonial movement, formed in the early 1940s. Its primary role was to express dissent through art, using folk theatre, songs, and plays to raise social and political awareness among the masses against British imperialism and feudal oppression.

questions.question: “Swadesh Sevak Home” is related to which of the following national freedom initiatives?
questions.solution:
Swadesh Sevak Home

The Swadesh Sevak Home was established in Vancouver, Canada, by G.D. Kumar. It was closely associated with the Ghadar Movement and served as a center for revolutionary activities and education for Indian immigrants, primarily Sikhs, to prepare them for the struggle against British rule in India.

questions.question: The period from 1905 to 1917 is known as the "Extremist Phase" in the Indian National Movement. Which of the following leaders was NOT associated with the extremist ideology during this time?
questions.solution:
Gopal Krishna Gokhale

Gopal Krishna Gokhale was a leading figure of the Moderate phase. He believed in constitutional methods, dialogue, and persuasion. In contrast, the 'Extremists' like Tilak, Pal, and Rai advocated for more radical actions like strikes and boycotts.

questions.question: During the third leg of Navika Sagar Parikrama II, which significant milestone did INSV Tarini achieve in January 2025?
questions.option_1: Docked at Port Stanley for the first time
questions.option_2: Completed a full circumnavigation of Antarctica
questions.option_4: Crossed Point Nemo, the Oceanic Pole of Inaccessibility
questions.solution:
INSV Tarini and Point Nemo

During the Navika Sagar Parikrama II in January 2025, the Indian Naval Sailing Vessel (INSV) Tarini reached Point Nemo. Point Nemo is known as the 'Oceanic Pole of Inaccessibility,' the place on Earth farthest from any landmass.

questions.question: Who among the following spearheaded an active non-cooperation movement famously known as the ‘Barasat Rebellion’ against the British indigo planters?
questions.solution:
Barasat Rebellion and Titu Mir

Titu Mir (Sayyid Mir Nisar Ali) led the Barasat Rebellion in 1831. While it began as a movement against the oppressive taxes levied by Hindu Zamindars, it turned into a fierce struggle against the British indigo planters and the colonial administration in Bengal.

questions.question: Jyotiba Phule was remembered on his birth anniversary on 11ᵗʰ April 2025 for which of the following major contributions to Indian society?
questions.option_4: Founding the first girls’ school in India
questions.solution:
Jyotiba Phule's Contribution

Mahatma Jyotiba Phule was a pioneer of women's education in India. Along with his wife Savitribai Phule, he founded the first school for girls in Pune (Bhide Wada) in 1848. He worked tirelessly for the upliftment of the oppressed castes and formed the Satyashodhak Samaj.

questions.question: The Mahalwari system was prevalent in certain regions of northern and central India. From whom was the land revenue collected under the Mahalwari system?
questions.solution:
The Mahalwari System

Introduced by Holt Mackenzie in 1822, the Mahalwari system treated the 'Mahal' (the entire village or estate) as a unit for revenue assessment. The responsibility for collecting and paying land revenue to the British was given to the village headman (Lambardar) rather than individual peasants.

questions.question: Which Indian freedom fighter was one of the first to openly support Marxist and communist ideologies, and played a pioneering role in the labour movement and the formation of the Communist Party of India?
questions.solution:
M.N. Roy

Manabendra Nath Roy (M.N. Roy) was a revolutionary philosopher and the primary founder of the Communist Party of India (CPI) in Tashkent in 1920. He was a pioneer in bringing Marxist ideology to India and was heavily involved in the international communist movement.

questions.question: The first Law Commission in India was headed by ______.
questions.solution:
First Law Commission

The first Law Commission of India was established in 1834 under the Charter Act of 1833. It was headed by Lord Macaulay. The commission's primary task was to codify Indian laws, which eventually led to the creation of the Indian Penal Code (IPC).

questions.question: Public health services remained largely inaccessible to the masses during the colonial rule. What was India's approximate life expectancy at the time of independence?
questions.solution:
Life Expectancy at Independence

Due to poor nutrition, frequent famines, and the near-absence of public healthcare during the colonial era, the approximate life expectancy in India at the time of independence (1947) was extremely low, around 30-32 years.

questions.question: Education remained largely inaccessible to the masses during colonial rule. What was India’s literacy rate around 1947?
questions.solution:
Literacy Rate in 1947

Colonial education policy focused on creating a small class of English-speaking clerks rather than mass education. Consequently, at the time of independence in 1947, India’s literacy rate was below 20% (approximately 12% to 16%).

questions.question: Which moderate leader, often referred to as the 'Grand Old Man of India,' became the first Indian elected to the British House of Commons and helped shape the early nationalist movement?
questions.solution:
The Grand Old Man of India

Dadabhai Naoroji is celebrated as the 'Grand Old Man of India'. He was the first Indian to be elected to the British House of Commons (1892). He is also famous for his 'Drain of Wealth' theory, explaining how Britain was economically exploiting India.

questions.question: Morarji Desai was commemorated on his 129ᵗʰ birth anniversary, 29 February 2025, for his contributions to governance reforms in India. Which of the following commissions did he chair in 1966 to restructure the country’s governance?
questions.solution:
1st Administrative Reforms Commission (ARC)

In 1966, the Government of India set up the 1st Administrative Reforms Commission to examine the public administration system. Morarji Desai was its initial chairman (later succeeded by K. Hanumanthaiah). The commission aimed to make the government more efficient and responsive.

questions.question: What was the main focus of the Aligarh Movement?
questions.solution:
The Aligarh Movement

The Aligarh Movement, led by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan, aimed at the social, political, and educational upliftment of Muslims in India. Its central focus was the promotion of modern Western education combined with Islamic values, leading to the foundation of the Mohammadan Anglo-Oriental College (now Aligarh Muslim University).

questions.question: In which year did the English East India Company pass a new law that required every new person who took up employment in the Company’s army to serve overseas if required?
questions.solution:
General Service Enlistment Act, 1856

The General Service Enlistment Act was passed in 1856 by Lord Canning. It mandated that all new recruits to the Bengal Army must be prepared to serve anywhere, including overseas. This caused great resentment among upper-caste sepoys, as crossing the ocean (Kala Pani) was considered a loss of caste in traditional beliefs.
